White shapes move with the blowing snow, visible only when they cross dark stone. The wolves circle wide before rushing from three sides.
A polar bear stands over a cracked breathing hole, hungry and unwilling to yield the ice. It roars when anyone approaches the water.
A playful squall twists into sharp little faces that spit sleet and laughter. The mephits dart between snow gusts until chased off.
White ground and white sky blur together until every direction feels the same. Reading wind crust and shadow keeps the party from walking in circles.
A sheet of snow hides a blue crack in the ice. It gives with a brittle pop under the first careless step.
Steam rises from a small spring where mineral water keeps the snow melted. Resting in the warmth brings feeling back to stiff fingers.
The curved runner of a sled juts from a drift beside scattered harness bells. Digging deeper may reveal cargo, survivors, or why they left the trail.
A cairn burns with blue fire that gives no heat. The flame leans toward a distant landmark whenever the wind dies.
A hunter's spear stands upright in the snow with no tracks around it. The shaft hums faintly when pointed toward the north.
Green-black lichen grows on stones beneath shallow snow. It is bitter but nourishing when scraped clean.
Crowberries cling to low shrubs in a sheltered hollow. The dark fruit freezes hard but keeps well.
A tuft of gray-green herbs grows where a warm rock melts the frost. The leaves must be clipped before they freeze again.
Dark char circle beneath a hole cut through clear ice. Keeping the hole open is as important as setting the line.
A meltwater stream runs just long enough for trout to gather in a gravel pool. The chance ends when night freezes the surface.
Small crabs cling under sea ice where the tide breathes through a crack. A hook can bring them up before fingers go numb.
A glacier has dropped iron-dark stones in a moraine ridge. Useful pieces must be sorted from frost-cracked rock.
Blue crystals grow in a sheltered ice cave where light enters from below. They shatter under sudden warmth or force.
A smooth soapstone outcrop rises from frozen ground, soft enough to carve but brittle in deep cold. Cutting blocks takes patience.
Dwarf birch grows low under snow crust, twisted but dry enough for fuel. Gathering it means finding the buried stems first.
Sea ice has pushed driftwood high onto the shore. The salt-dried logs are heavy but valuable where trees are rare.
A broken sled lies half frozen into a drift, its runners cracked but its boards usable. Freeing them without splintering takes careful wedges.
